Category.Name: Consistency
Category.Description: Ensures the model adheres to company standards, maintains accuracy across various checks, and minimizes inconsistencies in feature usage and parameter definitions.

Category.Name: Model Complexity
Category.Description: A high, medium, or low complexity score indicates the overall intricacy of the model. Complexity can stem from the number of features, dependencies, or design decisions. While not inherently good or bad, complex models may present maintenance challenges and inefficiencies. Opportunities to simplify models should be evaluated to streamline processes.

Category.Name: Model Integrity
Category.Description: Assesses the structural soundness of the model, ensuring that all features are well-formed, without errors, and that relationships between parts and assemblies are robust.

Category.Name: Design Intent Strength
Category.Description: Measures how effectively the model captures design intent, ensuring that relationships between dimensions, constraints, and features are robust and can handle modifications without failure. Proper design intent facilitates model changes and reduces future engineering costs.

Category.Name: Geometry Quality
Category.Description: Evaluates the mathematical accuracy and validity of the model's geometry, ensuring that it is reliable for downstream processes such as manufacturing, verification, or simulation. Good geometry quality prevents errors from cascading into subsequent stages of product development.

Check.Name: Accuracy Information
Check.Description: Checks the accuracy information of the model. Maintaining high accuracy is essential for ensuring that the model performs well in simulations and manufacturing processes. Proper accuracy management helps prevent errors and ensures the model meets design specifications. This check ensures that the specified accuracy is appropriate for the model's intended use.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why:
Relative accuracy puts your models at risk for both geometry and regeneration issues. Consider switching to absolute accuracy to avoid the potential issues caused by relative accuracy.
Using relative accuracy allows regeneration calculations to execute quickly and use minimal memory. While relative accuracy helps performance, it makes assumptions about the size of the geometry in your model that are sometimes invalid. If the assumptions are invalid, your models may have geom checks and/or regeneration issues that can be fixed by switching to absolute accuracy.
This Model Analysis considers relative accuracy a risk because of the strong correlation between relative accuracy and both geometry and regeneration issues. If you do not have either geometry or regeneration issues, then using relative accuracy may be acceptable. You must find an accuracy setting that balances acceptable geometry with regeneration performance, regardless of whether you are using relative or absolute accuracy. The lower the accuracy value, the finer the accuracy. Conversely, the higher the accuracy value, the coarser the accuracy. If the accuracy value is too coarse, the calculated geometry may be inaccurate. If the accuracy value is too fine, however, the memory consumption and regeneration time of your models may become unacceptable.
You need to take the geometry factor into account when making your accuracy setting. When your model is regenerated, the accuracy setting you provide gets multiplied by a geometry factor. The geometry factor is typically 3, but can grow to 10 depending on the features of your model. If you are using relative accuracy, you must consider the bounding box diagonal length when making your accuracy settings.
**Note** that coarse accuracy settings often lead to inaccurate calculations, but this is not always the case. If you have reviewed the geometry and do not observe geom checks or regeneration issues, then the coarse accuracy setting may be acceptable.

Check.Name: Additional Check Parameters
Check.Description: Ensures that additional check parameters are included. Proper management of check parameters helps in maintaining model integrity and ensuring that all necessary checks are performed. This is crucial for maintaining the overall quality of the model and preventing potential issues during downstream processes.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN N N N N Y

Check.Name: Buried Features
Check.Description: Identifies features that are buried within other geometry, making them inaccessible. Ensuring all features are accessible is important for both manufacturing and maintenance processes, preventing potential errors and rework. This check helps to identify and correct buried features to ensure proper model functionality.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E E E Y
Check.why:
A buried feature has geometry that is entirely surrounded (or enclosed) by another feature’s geometry. For example, a cut feature may be completely filled by one or more features. Or a solid feature, such as a protrusion, may be completely surrounded by geometry from one or more other features.
The design intent of a buried feature should be questioned since it is being replaced by geometry from other features.
Often buried features indicate a model that was modified when undesired geometry was cut away or an undesired region was filled in to start over. This may feel like a pragmatic solution, but it is only a short term solution. When the undesired design intent is left behind (or buried by geometry from new features), it makes the model unnecessarily complex. Good design intent should be understandable and the designer should make changes by modifying and redesigning the existing design intent. Leaving features that do not contribute to the design intent is confusing for new designers. ### Cuts outside the model: A cut outside the model is a cut feature that does not remove any material. The design intent of a cut that does not remove material should be questioned. Cuts outside the model are often caused by over-built sketches for cuts that intersect and contribute to the model’s geometry. In the current model state these sketches have no effect. You should express the design intent in a way that satisfies all potential input cases, since this ensures each feature has a purpose and that it creates the desired geometry based on the design intent. ### Unattached features: An unattached feature creates solid material that is not connected to the main solid. All parts should have no more than one solid body. If more than one solid body is present, this may indicate that you need an assembly.
Note that sometimes unattached features are acceptable, because later features connect the solids so the part has a single solid volume. Similar to cuts outside the model, unattached features are often caused by over-built geometry that sometimes intersects the main solid, although in the current state it does not. Consider redesigning the feature with references such asup to surfaceso the solid material always intersects the model. Or perhaps you can add a sketch reference that ensures the sketch is attached to the main solid.

Check.Name: Chamfer Child Features
Check.Description: Checks for chamfer features that are children of other features. Proper organization of chamfer features helps maintain model clarity and prevents errors during modifications. This check ensures that chamfers are appropriately placed and referenced within the model structure.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why: Creating child features that reference chamfered features is problematic because it can lead to unstable models and complicate future design changes. Chamfers should be added later in the model tree to maintain the integrity and flexibility of the design, ensuring that primary features remain robust and modifications are easier to manage.

Check.Name: Child Features Existence
Check.Description: Verifies that all child features exist and are correctly referenced. Properly managing child features ensures the stability and integrity of the model, reducing the risk of errors during design changes. This check helps maintain the hierarchical structure of the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y E E Y

Check.Name: Cosmetic Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of cosmetic features in the model. Managing cosmetic features effectively ensures that the model remains visually accurate and functional without unnecessary complexity. This check helps to identify and manage cosmetic features to maintain model clarity.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Cylindrical Cut Slots
Check.Description: Identifies cylindrical cut slots in the model. Ensuring the correct use of cylindrical cut slots helps maintain design intent and manufacturing feasibility. This check verifies that cylindrical cut slots are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Cylindrical Diameters
Check.Description: Checks the diameters of cylindrical features. Properly managing cylindrical diameters ensures that the model meets design specifications and is manufacturable. This check verifies that all cylindrical diameters are correctly defined and within specified tolerances.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Feature Renaming
Check.Description: Verifies that datum features are correctly renamed. Consistent and descriptive naming of datum features aids in model clarity and communication among design team members. This check ensures that datum features are appropriately named according to company standards.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Material Density Definition
Check.Description: Checks for the definition of material density in the model. Ensuring that material density is properly defined is crucial for accurate mass calculations and simulations. This check verifies that the material density is specified and correct for all materials used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why: The model density is necessary to evaluate the mass properties of a solid model. The default density is often 1.0, and it is highly unlikely this is valid for a real material. If your model’s mass properties are important, then the density should be set to the appropriate value of the intended material.

Check.Name: Default Child Features
Check.Description: Verifies that default child features are present and correctly referenced. Properly managing default child features helps maintain model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check ensures that all necessary default child features are included in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Draft Angles
Check.Description: Checks the draft angles of features. Ensuring correct draft angles is important for manufacturability, especially for molded or cast parts. This check verifies that all draft angles are correctly defined and meet manufacturing requirements.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Draft angles are crucial in injection molded parts to facilitate easy ejection, reduce mold wear, improve surface finish, maintain part integrity, simplify mold design, lower production costs, enhance part appearance and functionality, and ensure efficient manufacturing with typical angles ranging from 1 to 3 degrees depending on material and part geometry.

Check.Name: Draft Child Features
Check.Description: Verifies that draft features are correctly referenced as child features. Proper organization of draft features helps maintain model clarity and functionality. This check ensures that draft features are appropriately placed and referenced within the model structure.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Draft features can vary significantly as both their dimensions and their parent’s dimensions change. Because of the potentially significant changes in their geometry, it is unwise to reference their edges or surfaces. Since the internal structure and nature of the draft’s geometry can vary significantly with small changes, referencing the draft’s geometry is unreliable if changes are anticipated. Drafts are similarly complex features whose resulting geometry can vary significantly with small changes.

Check.Name: Datum Axes Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of datum axes. Properly managing datum axes ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all datum axes are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Coordinate Systems Information
Check.Description: Verifies the information of coordinate systems. Proper management of coordinate systems is essential for accurate assembly and manufacturing processes. This check ensures that all coordinate systems are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Curves Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of datum curves. Proper management of datum curves ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all datum curves are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Planes Information
Check.Description: Verifies the information of datum planes. Proper management of datum planes is essential for accurate assembly and manufacturing processes. This check ensures that all datum planes are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Points Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of datum points. Properly managing datum points ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all datum points are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Duplicate Models
Check.Description: Identifies duplicate models in the database. Managing duplicate models helps maintain the integrity of the database and prevents confusion during design processes. This check helps to identify and manage duplicates to ensure database consistency.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Early Chamfer Features
Check.Description: Checks for chamfer features created early in the model history. Ensuring chamfers are added at the correct stage of design helps maintain model integrity and functionality. This check verifies that chamfers are appropriately timed in the design process.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Using chamfers too early in the design model tree can lead to significant problems, particularly if they are used as references for constructing other features, because this practice can compromise the integrity of the model, complicate future design modifications, and create dependencies that make the design less flexible and more difficult to manage.

Check.Name: Early Cosmetic Features
Check.Description: Verifies that cosmetic features are added at the correct stage of design. Proper management of cosmetic features helps maintain model integrity and functionality. This check ensures that cosmetic features are appropriately timed in the design process.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Early Draft Features
Check.Description: Checks for draft features created early in the model history. Ensuring drafts are added at the correct stage of design helps maintain model integrity and functionality. This check verifies that drafts are appropriately timed in the design process.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Using drafts too early in the design model tree can lead to significant problems, particularly if they are used as references for constructing other features, because this practice can compromise the integrity of the model, complicate future design modifications, and create dependencies that make the design less flexible and more difficult to manage.

Check.Name: Early Round Features
Check.Description: Verifies that round features are added at the correct stage of design. Proper management of round features helps maintain model integrity and functionality. This check ensures that rounds are appropriately timed in the design process.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Edge References
Check.Description: Checks for references to edges. Proper management of edge references ensures model stability and reduces the risk of errors during design changes. This check verifies that edge references are appropriately managed within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why:
Using edge references in a model can lead to significant problems, such as increased susceptibility to errors when geometry changes, difficulty in maintaining design intent, and challenges in updating the model, as edge references can create unstable dependencies that compromise the robustness of the design.
Edge references are not recommended since future changes to the design intent may cause the edge to disappear and in turn, the feature that references the now non-existant edge will likely fail. An edge is one type of geometric entity that represents the boundary of an internal surface or face on a solid, or the boundary of a surface or a surface’s internal patch. If an edge disappears, then features that referenced the edge will fail to regenerate. Because of the risk of an edge disappearing, edges are usually considered unstable and weak choices for references.
Always consider alternate reference types before selecting an edge. Usually an alternate reference such as a surface or an intent object are more robust. Ensure you do not accidentally select an edge when another type of reference would be more robust under change. It is a good practice to use the Query Select option when selecting a reference and to carefully observe the selection. Fortunately, beginning with Pro/ENGINEER Wildfire 4.0 surfaces are selected by default, reducing the chance of accidentally selecting an edge.
If you feel an edge reference is appropriate and necessary:
- Understand that it may be unstable under change and you risk experiencing regeneration errors about missing references after making a change. - Limit 1by1 edge selections and instead prefer intent objects or rule based selection such as tangent chain, boundary chain, surface loop, or from-to chain. Intent objects and rule based selections minimize the number of references and can be more robust under change. - Use care when sequencing the order of features in the model tree. For example, round features that reference edges should occur before any feature that may consume edges it references. - When changes are required to a sketch that creates edges that are referenced downstream, ensure you replace the sketched entities rather than simply deleting and adding entities. When the Replace operation is used, downstream references are rerouted automatically.

Check.Name: Extra Layers
Check.Description: Identifies extra layers in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y
Check.why:
Layers are used in models and drawings to control which view items are displayed. Models and drawings with many pieces of information can be difficult to work with if all of the information is turned on at the same time.
By organizing view items into different layers, you can select which layers are displayed, hidden, or isolated so you can focus on the desired information. Understanding the number of layers in your models and the occurrence of layers without entities is a good starting point for assessing how effectively you are using layers. A high level view of how layers are being used is also helpful for very specialized types of CAD data conversion projects.
**Note:** Layers can only organize view items such as datums, surface quilts, and components. The hiding or isolation of specific features within a part’s solid or within a surface’s quilt is not possible with layers. Instead, simplified representations should be created to control the presence of specific features.

Check.Name: Extra Parameters
Check.Description: Checks for extra parameters in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Including extra parameters in a model can lead to significant problems, such as increased complexity, potential for errors, confusion during modifications, and difficulties in maintaining the model, all of which can hinder efficient design and development.

Check.Name: Family Table Information
Check.Description: Verifies the information of family tables.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Feature Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of features. Proper management of features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all features are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: File Size
Check.Description: Verifies the file size of the model. Managing file size helps ensure that the model is optimized for performance and storage. This check ensures that the model file size is within acceptable limits for performance and storage.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Freeform Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of freeform features.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Feature Template Default Values
Check.Description: Verifies the default values of feature templates.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Feature Template Standard Parameters
Check.Description: Checks the standard parameters of feature templates.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Geometric Checks
Check.Description: Performs geometric checks on the model. Ensuring geometric integrity is crucial for accurate manufacturing and simulation results. This check verifies that all geometric features are correctly defined and meet design specifications.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N E Y
Check.why:
Geom checks should be removed from all models that will be reused or referenced in downstream work. Sometimes resolving geom checks causes new geom checks or regeneration issues to appear. This occurs when the original geom check masked or cloaked downstream problems. If this is the case, it can be difficult and potentially impractical to fix the geom checks if they went unnoticed or were ignored for a long time. This is why it is important to resolve geom checks sooner rather than later.
Geom checks may propagate through the dependency chain Models or drawings that reference models with geom checks risk inheriting a similar geometry issue that can become more serious. Even if the model with a geom check is a distant ancestor and not an immediate parent, it may be possible for the geom check to skip a generation and propagate to the child or grandchild.
### Geom checks are often caused by: - Regeneration issues - Inappropriate model accuracy settings - Import features - Merge or cutout features - External copy geometry features - Copy geometry features
### Resolving geom checks Since an issue may propagate through the associative chain of references between models, first focus on the models at the top of the dependency chain. Beginning with the isolated occurrences is a good strategy because fixing them may also resolve the geom checks in their downstream models.

Check.Name: Geometric Tolerances
Check.Description: Checks the geometric tolerances of features. Properly managing geometric tolerances ensures that the model meets design specifications and is manufacturable. This check verifies that all geometric tolerances are correctly defined and within specified limits.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Hole Diameters
Check.Description: Verifies the diameters of hole features. Properly managing hole diameters ensures that the model meets design specifications and is manufacturable. This check ensures that all hole diameters are correctly defined and within specified limits.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Ignored Features
Check.Description: Checks for features marked to be ignored in the model. Properly managing ignored features ensures model clarity and prevents unnecessary complexity. This check helps to identify and manage ignored features to maintain model clarity.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Imported Features
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of imported features. Properly managing imported features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all imported features are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Incomplete Features
Check.Description: Checks for incomplete features in the model. Ensuring all features are complete is crucial for maintaining model integrity and preventing errors during manufacturing and simulation. This check helps to identify and correct incomplete features in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Insert Mode Usage
Check.Description: Verifies the use of insert mode in the model. Properly managing insert mode helps maintain model integrity and functionality during design changes. This check ensures that insert mode is used appropriately within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Instance Naming
Check.Description: Checks the naming of instances in the model. Properly naming instances helps maintain model clarity and prevents confusion during design processes. This check ensures that all instances are correctly named according to company standards.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Layer Display Status
Check.Description: Verifies the display status of layers. Properly managing layer display status helps maintain model clarity and prevents unnecessary complexity. This check ensures that all layers have appropriate display settings.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y

Check.Name: Multiple Layer Items
Check.Description: Ensures that specific items or features (such as dimensions, annotations, datums, etc.) are placed on designated layers in your model. This is useful for maintaining organized and standardized models where items are systematically managed and easily accessible through layers.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why: Layers are used in models and drawings to control which view items are displayed. Models and drawings with many pieces of information can be difficult to work with if all of the information is turned on at the same time. By organizing view items into different layers, you can select which layers are displayed, hidden, or isolated so you can focus on the desired information. Understanding the number of layers in your models and the occurrence of layers without entities is a good starting point for assessing how effectively you are using layers. A high level view of how layers are being used is also helpful for very specialized types of CAD data conversion projects. Note: Layers can only organize view items such as datums, surface quilts, and components. The hiding or isolation of specific features within a part’s solid or within a surface’s quilt is not possible with layers. Instead, simplified representations should be created to control the presence of specific features.

Check.Name: Blank Datum Layers
Check.Description: Checks for blank datum layers. Properly managing datum layers ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all datum layers are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y
Check.why: Blank datum layers in a model can lead to significant problems, including difficulty in understanding the model structure, challenges in making accurate modifications, and potential errors in downstream processes, as hidden datums can obscure the true design intent and dependencies.

Check.Name: Unused Parameters
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of unused parameters. Properly managing parameters helps maintain model clarity and prevents unnecessary complexity. This check helps to identify and manage unused parameters to maintain model simplicity and clarity.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Unused parameters in a model can lead to confusion, increased complexity, and potential errors during the design process. Ensuring that all parameters are necessary and utilized helps maintain model clarity, simplifies modifications, and enhances the overall efficiency and accuracy of the design.

Check.Name: Incomplete References
Check.Description: Checks for incomplete references in the model. Properly managing references ensures model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check helps to identify and correct incomplete references within the model.

Check.Name: Required Features
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of required features in the model. Properly managing required features ensures model integrity and functionality. This check ensures that all required features are present and correctly defined in the model.

Check.Name: Regeneration Errors
Check.Description: Checks for regeneration errors in the model. Properly managing regeneration errors ensures model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check helps to identify and correct regeneration errors within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why:
Regeneration errors in a model are far more severe than warnings, as they can halt the design process entirely, cause loss of data, create significant errors in the model, and necessitate extensive troubleshooting and corrections, severely impacting the reliability and progress of the design workflow.
Regeneration issues in a model can cause **significant problems**, such as disrupting the design process, introducing errors, reducing model reliability, and complicating troubleshooting efforts, all of which can hinder the efficiency and accuracy of the overall design workflow.
Retrieval and regeneration messages should be reviewed carefully because of their potential impact in downstream use. Note that it is possible for issues to skip a generation and propagate to a model’s grandchildren or more distant descendants, while the model’s direct children appear to have no issues at all.
Some types of retrieval and regeneration issues are more serious than others. Consider how you will use the model in downstream work when deciding whether to fix its retrieval and regeneration issues. Retrieval and regeneration issues in models with family tables are more serious than those in non-family table models because will propagate to each instance in the family.
### Types of regeneration issues: - Regeneration failed - Failed relations - Missing and invalid references - Circular references - Design intent unclear warnings - Model changed since mass properties calculated - Frozen components - Incomplete features or components - Missing models - Design changes in upstream models and/or features - Selection of unstable/unreliable references - Geometry issues with referenced geometry - General error messages

Check.Name: Regeneration Warnings
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of regeneration warnings in the model. Properly managing regeneration warnings helps maintain model integrity and functionality. This check helps to identify and manage regeneration warnings within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why:
Regeneration issues in a model can cause significant problems, such as disrupting the design process, introducing errors, reducing model reliability, and complicating troubleshooting efforts, all of which can hinder the efficiency and accuracy of the overall design workflow.
Retrieval and regeneration messages should be reviewed carefully because of their potential impact in downstream use. Note that it is possible for issues to skip a generation and propagate to a model’s grandchildren or more distant descendants, while the model’s direct children appear to have no issues at all.
Some types of retrieval and regeneration issues are more serious than others. Consider how you will use the model in downstream work when deciding whether to fix its retrieval and regeneration issues. Retrieval and regeneration issues in models with family tables are more serious than those in non-family table models because will propagate to each instance in the family.
### Types of regeneration issues: - Regeneration failed - Failed relations - Missing and invalid references - Circular references - Design intent unclear warnings - Model changed since mass properties calculated - Frozen components - Incomplete features or components - General error messages

Check.Name: Relation Comments
Check.Description: Checks for comments in relations. Properly managing relation comments helps maintain clarity and prevents confusion during design processes. This check ensures that all relations have appropriate comments for clarity and documentation.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Relation Errors
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of errors in relations. Properly managing relation errors ensures model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check helps to identify and correct errors within relations in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Description: Checks the information of relations. Properly managing relation information ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all relation information is correctly defined and used in the model.

Check.Name: Missing Relations
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of missing relations. Properly managing relations ensures model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check helps to identify and correct missing relations within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Multiple Relations
Check.Description: Checks for multiple instances of relations. Properly managing relations helps maintain clarity and prevents confusion during design processes. This check ensures that multiple instances of relations are correctly managed within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why: Similar to an equation in a spreadsheet, a relation is used to perform calculations and assign the result to a parameter or dimension of the model. Having many relations can lead to poor design intent if not well documented.

Check.Name: Renamed Symbols
Check.Description: Verifies the renaming of symbols. Properly managing symbol names helps maintain clarity and prevents confusion during design processes. This check ensures that all symbols are correctly renamed according to company standards.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Round Child Features
Check.Description: Verifies that round features do not have child features.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why: Round features can vary significantly as both their dimensions and their parent’s dimensions change. Because of the potentially significant changes in their geometry, it is unwise to reference their edges or surfaces. Since the internal structure and nature of the round’s geometry can vary significantly with small changes, referencing the geometry is unreliable if changes are anticipated. Rounds are similarly complex features whose resulting geometry can vary significantly with small changes.

Check.Name: Rule Check Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of rule checks. Properly managing rule checks ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check verifies that all rule check information is correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Sharp Edges
Check.Description: Identifies sharp edges in the model. Properly managing sharp edges helps ensure the model meets design specifications and is manufacturable. This check helps to identify and manage sharp edges to maintain model safety and manufacturability.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Short Edges
Check.Description: Checks for short edges in the model. Properly managing short edges ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check helps to identify and manage short edges within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why:
You must be sensitive to accuracy settings when you have small geometry relative to the overall size of the model. If the accuracy is not set appropriately, geom checks could make the geometry unreliable in downstream work, as well as cause regeneration issues.
When a model has small geometry, the equivalent absolute accuracy should be two to three times smaller than the shortest edge. Some features have edges that are short in length. The definition of "short" varies depending on the overall size of the model and the current accuracy settings. If there are edges that are very short given the current accuracy settings, they are identified by this report.
### Short edges can occur in many situations. Some common cases include: - Transition geometry for round features. - External models that were merged or cut out of the current model. - External geometry referenced using copy geom and external copy geom features. - Imported geometry

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Bend Tables
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of sheet metal bend tables. Properly managing sheet metal features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all sheet metal bend tables are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: Missing sheet metal bend tables in sheet metal parts can lead to significant issues, including inaccurate bend allowances, incorrect part dimensions, and manufacturing errors, all of which compromise the part's functionality and increase the risk of costly rework and delays in production.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Flat Patterns
Check.Description: Checks for flat pattern representations in sheet metal parts. This check ensures that all sheet metal flat patterns are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: The absence of sheet metal flat patterns in models can lead to significant problems, such as inaccuracies in the manufacturing process, difficulties in verifying part dimensions, and challenges in ensuring proper fit and function, ultimately compromising the efficiency and accuracy of production.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Thickness
Check.Description: Verifies the thickness of sheet metal parts. Properly managing sheet metal features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all sheet metal thicknesses are correctly defined and within specified limits.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Incorrect use of sheet metal thickness can lead to significant problems, such as structural weaknesses, fabrication errors, and improper fit, which can compromise model accuracy and hinder downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. Ensuring all sheet metal thicknesses are correctly defined and within specified limits is essential for maintaining the integrity and functionality of the design.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Unbend Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of unbend features in sheet metal parts. Properly managing sheet metal features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all unbend features are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: Missing unbend features in sheet metal parts can lead to significant issues, such as inaccuracies in flat pattern development, difficulties in verifying part dimensions, and challenges during manufacturing, all of which compromise the overall quality and functionality of the final product.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Y-Factor
Check.Description: Verifies the Y-factor for sheet metal parts. Properly managing sheet metal features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that the Y-factor is correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Incorrect Y-factor values in sheet metal parts can lead to inaccuracies in bend allowances, resulting in incorrect part dimensions and potential manufacturing errors. Ensuring the Y-factor is correctly defined is crucial for maintaining the precision and functionality of the final product.

Check.Name: Simplified Representation Information
Check.Description: Checks the information of simplified representations. Properly managing simplified representations ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all simplified representations are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Incorrectly defined or unused simplified representations in a model can cause misunderstandings, inaccuracies, and inefficiencies during the design process. Properly managing simplified representations helps organize work in large assemblies, reduces excess regeneration, and ensures clarity, accuracy, and efficiency in the design and review stages.

Check.Name: Simplified Representation Naming
Check.Description: Verifies the naming of simplified representations. Properly managing simplified representations helps maintain clarity and prevents confusion during design processes. This check ensures that all simplified representations are correctly named according to company standards.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Sketch Items
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of sketch items in the model. Properly managing sketch items ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all sketch items are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
A sketches geometry is calculated automatically by powerful algorithms that reason about the entities, constraints and dimensions in a sketch. Without Sketcher, the resulting geometry is difficult to determine. You must still perform some level of reasoning about a sketch’s design intent as you create the sketched entities and dimensioning scheme. However, you do not have to perform the actual calculations and you can use Sketcher’s proposed dimensioning scheme to guide you and verify the sketch is fully constrained and dimensioned. Since you still need to reason and verify the design intent, you should strive to make the sketches as simple as possible without compromising the design.
### Simplifying Sketches: One way to simplify sketches is to separate the design intent into different features. For example, rounds and draft features are often better than adding their detail into a sketch. Another way to simplify sketch geometry is to create the constraints before you create dimensions. Adding a constraint often reduces the total number of constraints and dimensions proposed by Sketcher’s intent manager.
Imported sketch geometry can lead to many sketched entities. Even if you have to import the geometry, you may not need to reconstruct its design intent in Sketcher. If you do need to capture the design intent, you can separate the section’s geometry across multiple features so it is easier to reason about and verify. If you do have to capture design intent with Sketcher, then you can import the geometry as a datum curve. Another common problem with imported sketch geometry is that it may have many small entities that could be merged and/or simplified with a single spline entity. Pre-processing geometry to reduce the number of entities can be helpful before importing it (this is common with imported logo geometry).

Check.Name: SLA Features Information
Check.Description: Verifies the information of SLA features. Properly managing SLA features ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all SLA features are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW N N N N Y

Check.Description: Identifies small cylindrical surfaces in the model. Properly managing small surfaces ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check helps to identify and manage small cylindrical surfaces within the model.

Check.Description: Checks for the presence of surface edges in the model. Properly managing surface edges ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all surface edges are correctly defined and used in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Start Part Checks
Check.Description: Verifies the presence of start part checks in the model. Properly managing start checks ensures model accuracy and facilitates downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. This check ensures that all start checks are correctly defined and used in the model.

Check.Name: Start Part Layer Check
Check.Description: This check is utilized to check for missing layers that should be part of the model, ensuring that the layers conform to the predefined standards or templates used by your organization.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y

Check.Name: Suppressed Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of suppressed features in the model. Properly managing suppressed features ensures model stability and prevents errors during design changes. This check helps to identify and manage suppressed features within the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y
Check.why:
Leaving features suppressed in models can lead to significant problems later, as it can cause confusion, disrupt the model's intended functionality, create errors during regeneration, and complicate future modifications by masking dependencies and design intent.
Suppressed features are appropriate if they are only used by particular instances of the model, or for a simplified representation. Suppressed features in a master representation without a family table, however, indicate a problematic design intent that was suppressed temporarily. If the suppressed features are no longer needed, then they should be removed before you release the model. The presence of suppressed features adds confusion to your models, a well as runs the risk of having the features unintentionally resumed (or unsuppressed) some time in the future.
